summary:
The Safavid-Ottoman Wars (1514–1639 AD) were a series of military conflicts between the [[event:2338|Safavid Empire]] of Persia and the [[event:2400|Ottoman Empire]] — the defining rivalry of the early modern Middle East, fought along a contested frontier stretching from Anatolia through Iraq to the Persian Gulf, and driven simultaneously by geopolitical competition for Mesopotamian and Caucasian territories and by the profound sectarian divide between Ottoman Sunni Islam and Safavid Shia Islam. The wars began with the [[event:2514|Battle of Chaldiran]] (1514) and continued with varying intensity for 125 years, consuming the resources of both empires, devastating the populations of the contested borderlands, and ultimately producing the Treaty of Zuhab (1639) which established a frontier roughly corresponding to the modern Iran-Iraq border.

The Safavid-Ottoman rivalry shaped the Middle East's sectarian geography in ways that persist to the present day. Shah Ismail I's imposition of Twelver Shia Islam on Persia — combined with Ottoman Sultan Selim I's campaign against Shia populations in Anatolia that he regarded as a potential fifth column — created a deep Sunni-Shia geopolitical fault line across the region. The wars also had strategic consequences for Europe: the constant Ottoman military commitment on the eastern front limited the resources available for campaigns in Europe, contributing to the Ottoman failure to take Vienna in 1529 ([[event:2323|Siege of Vienna]]). For Persia, the wars required military modernization and alliance-seeking with European powers — Portuguese, Spanish, and British — who shared an interest in checking Ottoman power.
